He almost always responded to posts on piazza within 10 minutes. I think if grading expectations for testing was more clear, I would have performed better with testing and debugging my scripts. I would have run into seg faults less if I could figure out in what place of code causes these seg faults in the automated  hidden grading tests. I also loved how he would listen to students' concerns and be reasonable about deadlines. He was really fair overall and wanted to make sure we all do well, and learn a lot that is relevant for computer science careers, beyond academia.", "He provided alternative wordings when ESL students did not follow his meaning.  Otherwise when the meaning was clear but a student was having difficulty grasping the core concept he would pause to recap the topic and he would offer to cover it again during a class break if the student was still unsure before moving forward in the lesson plan.He showed consideration and additional flexibility for students who had family emergencies, grieving, or health issues.  Of course he still required the assignments to be complete but the willingness to extend deadlines was notable and helped to keep students motivated to learn despite challenges.  I am very grateful for the leniency he provided for deadlines while I was grieving the loss of a family member.  I didn't want to withdraw the course and still felt up to learning and completing the assignments.  However, I was having some difficulties completing some of the assignments on time and he was willing to accept late work in some cases to ensure I could continue progressing through the course."]

comments_json: ["This course covers a wide range of database–related topics, but there is significant room for improvement in teaching pace, tool introduction, and project guidance:1. Unreasonable teaching sequence: Week 7 directly presents the finished website for Practicum 1 (PHP implementation), but more fundamental topics like Relational Algebra and Datalog are taught later, causing difficulties in understanding and keeping up.3. Insufficient instruction in later weeks: Weeks 11–14 have  no formal lectures, with weeks 11 and 13 being only in–class assignments, week 12 being office hours, and week 14 being Practicum 2 presentations. There is a lack of systematic explanation of key content.4. Resource integration issues: Video resources on Canvas contain content from different schools (CMU, Berkeley，thanks for offering the chances to get Ivy League  experience in NEU anyway ) with some duplication, without proper screening and organization for this course's content, adding information noise.5. Project–curriculum disconnect: Practicum 2 involves features like Indexing and Transactions that are not covered in the course(at least not in in–person class) yet students are required to implement them in projects, forcing extensive self–exploration.6. Lack of full–stack guidance: The course doesn't require  prerequisites but also doesn't provide guidance for building full–stack projects from scratch. It simply suggests/forces using AI, completely lacking instruction on development processes and technology selection.It is recommended that the course add systematic introductions to tools and environments at the beginning, adjust the sequence of theory and practice, ensure that skills needed for projects are explained before formal assignments, organize and streamline external resources, and provide minimal working project examples with step–by–step guidance for students without full–stack backgrounds.", "The Instructor was ready to provide assistance where needed. course_info: - TRACE report for CS5800-01 Algorithms  (Jesse Stern)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Never teach any course again", "By far my biggest complaint with Professor Stern is the abundance of typos in his lectures and assignments. Nearly every week, he would need to clarify details about assignments or lectures because of these mistakes. That being said, he was very quick to address these typos and would always provide clear follow –up explanations. However, these typos sometimes affected our understanding of course material or would change our original approaches to homework assignments. I think simply proofreading his work before publishing would go a long way!", "A few modules used videos from past semesters by other lecturer, which I found to be less helpful and hard to understand – I wish the instructor had time to curate videos for all modules.", "While Prof. Stern's enthusiasm, ability to clear up confusion throughout the course, and availability was evident and greatly appreciated, I would recommend that assignments are thoroughly reviewed before being published. Due to the nature of an algorithms course, even the slightest errors in assignment problems can lead to confusion. I noticed that I ended up spending an average of 1–2 additional hours per week just because of typos that were made in assignments. Sometimes these typos make problems simpler, but often times the problems became more difficult and required additional hints. Sometimes I would complete a problem, and a week later I would check Piazza and see that there was an error in the problem which means I had to go back and fix my solution on many occasions.Additionally, Prof. Stern's videos were much more informative than some of the videos from other resources that he used, so I would encourage him to strictly use his own lectures. Sometimes when videos were used from other professors, there seemed to be mixed messaging or simply just different teaching styles, which can throw students off in complex courses.I loved Prof. Stern's teaching style and enthusiasm, and I think the only things he could do to make the course better are to reduce typos that lead to increased  time spent on problems, as well as strictly using his own videos in lecture. I think this would lead to a more fluid course with less confusion. It's evident how much Prof. Stern cares about his course.", "•\tProvide concise written summaries or supplementary notes alongside videos so students can quickly reference concepts without re–watching full lectures.•\tCalibrate the communication around academic integrity to feel less accusatory e.g., initial outreach could invite dialogue instead of presuming guilt.•\tIntroduce early low–stakes feedback opportunities (e.g., a check–in assignment) to surface misunderstandings before high–impact consequences occur.•\tOffer clearer examples of acceptable vs. unacceptable use of external tools (like LLMs) and model consistent behavior to avoid perceptions of double standards.•\tEncourage a learning culture where students feel safe to ask questions and demonstrate partial understanding without fear of immediate severe penalties.", "I hope he teaches via Teams or Zoom.", "Replace the old course videos ASAP. Get rid of the final group project. Just adds extra stress without any additional learning and just feels like a waste of time.", "Create more activities apart from course", "Instructor could really include more essential topics like trees in the course (which they didn't) and could try to be more friendly with students rather than scare them with formal tone or formal announcements. Syllabus literally scared me when I first read it, rather than a friendly suggestion. They could have mentioned the reason that why using LLM would not benefit students so that a normal student understands why they enforce these statements or rules in the class. It's good that we have such rules, but we can try to show more BTS in the syllabus."]

course_info: - TRACE report for CS5800-01 Algorithms  (Jesse Stern)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["Other than the typo issue mentioned above, I think Professor Stern did a great job. He was approachable, relatable, and transparent. Overall, great professor and interesting (albeit, very difficult) class!", "The instructor taught well, made videos that helped understand even extremely complex topics.", "•\tStrength: The strict sourcing policy creates a level playing field in theory everyone is held to the same standard around what counts as supported knowledge.•\tArea for improvement: The enforcement and tone around academic integrity disproportionately increased anxiety, especially among students who may already feel marginalized or uncertain; inclusive learning would benefit from clear, empathetic communication and scaffolds to help students meet expectations.•\tArea for improvement: The absence of multiple modalities (only videos, no written reference material) limits accessibility for example, students who process written explanations better or who need to quickly scan concepts were disadvantaged.•\tInclusive practice could be improved by offering more transparent rubrics, explicit examples, and early feedback loops so students from diverse backgrounds and preparation levels can orient themselves without fear.•\tCreating structured opportunities for peer discussion or sanctioned “office hours walk–throughs” could help students feel more supported rather than isolated.", "He is really good at explaining difficult materials easy.", "Super clear instruction, obviously knows what he's talking about, well laid out and easy to follow.", "My only recommendation would be to have ALL his videos done by him. course_info: - TRACE report for CS6120-02 Natural Language Processing  (Tala Talaei Khoei)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["To make the course better, Dr. Tala could do the following:1) Re–structure use of class time and PowerPoints. It'd be better to have 30 – 50 slides in a presentation, with thoughtful discussion on a topic, then trying to rush through > 100 slides each week2) Come up with assignments / datasets that work well for NLP. Not exaggerating... the best exercises in NLP that I did this semester came from the Udemy course I signed up for on the side. For certain tasks like word/sentence embedding, classification models, etc., there are simple and structured datasets like subsets of labelled twitter posts, etc. that were easy to use with, but our data on assignments was either too large for even Cloud GPUs to run effectively, or a small self–created 300 word PDF (per requirements) that didn't have enough info. to do meaningful data analysis on.3) Code simple examples herself that can actually be discussed / run in class, rather than pulling code screenshots from the internet and looking at screenshots of the output.4) Be intentional around break times, sometimes it felt like Tala wanted to get through lecture so she could end class early, and would skip hourly breaks that made the 100+ slides noted in 1) feel rougher.", "The instructor already did a great job, I don't think they could've done anything else.", "There need to be more opportunities for collective learning. The instructor needs to approach the course  and students as a group of capable graduate students. There was NO feedback on assignments so learning from assignments was limited. Way more feedback on assignments needed and collective reflection back on assignments to learn from one another different methods and reasoning for approaches and different outcomes.  More suggested reading material would be valuable. Course time should be used for building collective understanding vs didactic progression through 100+ slides with definitions.", "This class really struggled with effective lectures and  application. I felt myself really struggling with the real world application of this since we never saw how any of the theory was used.  The instructor would leave a few minutes at the end of class to look at the (mostly AI generated) code that rarely ran and was told to just \"look at it at home\" even if it took hours to run.  She wasnt able to answer simple questions in class in a useful manner. Everything I learned in this class I taught myself and feel ill prepared to apply any of this in the real world even if this was what I was looking forward to most in this program.   If this would have been my first roux class I would have dropped out of the program, it was a waste of time."]

course_info: - TRACE report for CS6120-02 Natural Language Processing  (Tala Talaei Khoei)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["Dr. Tala attempts to include students in the learning process, but it came across in the wrong way and actually made the learning environment feel hostile.1) For example, to get students to join the discussion, Dr. Tala will try to ask softball questions for people to answer, often times with the definition or answer to the question right on the current slide. Some example questions she asked in lecture include:- What is sentiment analysis?- What is sentiment?- What is a part of speech?It feels kind of absurd to be asked such simple questions in a masters program. Often times I'd feel something like \"are you just trying to get us to respond without asking a thoughtful question, or do you think we're this [Redacted] that we actually need to think about this question?\" Either way, I'm guessing that at least some other students felt a similar way about these kinds of questions, because often times nobody would want to respond to these kind of softballs, unless sometimes we'd try to answer just to keep the class moving.However, in the times when students didn't respond, Dr. Tala would say things like:- \"Come on guys, really, nobody is going to answer?...\"- \"Come on guys, the answer is right there...\"Which further made me feel like she either thought we were [Redacted], or was kind of antagonizing us. Either way, this pattern happened often and made class lectures slow down and feel super awkward.", "The instructor makes sure to teach the basics before getting into the advanced topics, she also clears everybody's doubts and gives ample opportunities to the students to express their doubts (if they have any) during class.  There are no areas of improvement that I can think of.", "The topic is extremely interesting and despite the poor course design, I'm still interested in it. The assignments were challenging and created opportunities for learning, but the lack of ANY feedback on assignments and any collective discussion results in the cycle of learning/the opportunity to learn from those assignments being missed. Did the instructor design and do the assignments? This would be essential to support deeper reflection on the assignments and in order to be able to give useful feedback. This course did not feel graduate level at all. At this level, I would suggest that peers should be part of the source of learning in addition to instructor, assignments, and materials. In addition, we should be exploring authentic questions. The instructor did not exhibit genuine interest in the material, in the learning of the community of students, and in learning as a complex cognitive process. As a graduate student who wants to engage deeply through exploration, discussion, debate, experimentation/testing ideas, asking questions, this class misses the mark by a wide margin. Roux/Northeastern need to better evaluate the courses and programs. If students are just here for a credential and if faculty are just here to shepherd students to that credential, the credential no longer means anything. For students here to learn, explore, and innovate at a graduate level, classes like this are missing the mark. This instructor and this course are not alone in this regard particularly in regard to lack of useful feedback in the cycle of learning. More evaluation at an institutional level is needed. More mentoring and  more support for new instructors are critical. Less reliance on graduate students for grading/feedback or better TAs would also be helpful. The organization also needs to listening to student feedback.", "More interaction in class using code would have been really beneficial. Hours of lecturing with 100+ slides that say mostly similar things and are just copied and pasted off other slides off the internet isn't conducive to learning.  Smaller assignments that were manageable would have really gone a long way here. Being told one of the assignments was \"easy and we were only looking at a few languages\" for that to not be the case and have a huge, computer crashing file with hundreds of languages didn't instill confidence that the instructor even KNEW what she was assigning most of the time. These assignments would take weeks to get through and then never mentioned again in class so got no direction if we were doing it correctly or learning what we should have been.   There could have been a really fun and interesting way to teach this class and we did not get that or the confidence that this instructor understood NLP or how to code it."]

comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["Many times we had to do online group work for math problems involving matrices using a Google Doc and trying to do this made me more confused because I would spend more time trying to format my work on the Google Doc instead of actually trying to understand and solve the problem. Multiple times content/information was released late on Canvas/Piazza and yet the students were still expected to understand everything for the weekly quiz as if they had nothing to do other than constantly monitor Canvas/Piazza."]

course_info: - TRACE report for CS7880-01 Spec Topic Theor Comp Sci  (Oscar Veliz)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Focus less on whether the students can do the math by hand and more so on making the program do it and understanding concepts and algorithms and limitations."]

course_info: - TRACE report for CS7880-01 Spec Topic Theor Comp Sci  (Oscar Veliz)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["This instructor was poorly prepared and clearly didn't understand some of the content he was teaching. When asked to further explain some concepts that weren't taught well he would often get defensive and make the student feel like it was their fault for not understanding, or he would just repeat exactly what he said already. He would often ask for feedback in assignments, but never actually made any changes. It was also very unclear what he would actually want you to take away from topics and therefore very hard to figure out what he might test you on. He often made excuses for why he was unprepared but just forced the students to pick up his slack. The study materials/homework he would give seemed like he didn't go through it himself and often was repetitive or didn't make sense. This was most evident in the in–class group work in which he clearly had no idea how much time should be allotted."]

course_info: - TRACE report for CS7880-01 Spec Topic Theor Comp Sci  (Oscar Veliz)
comment_prompt: What I could have done to make this course better for myself.
comments_json: ["I could have gone out of my way more to find material related to the topics we were supposed to cover in class because the coverage in class was often lacking."]

comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Where to begin... Professor Nafa would show up entirely unprepared for lecture. She would sometimes spend 40+ minutes at a time creating our assignments in silence during class time. Her blatant disregard for our time felt disrespectful, as there were some lectures we would spend the overwhelming majority of the class sitting in silence and staring at her blank screen. As I previously mentioned, the Canvas page and course materials were so unorganized it hindered my ability to follow the course. She did not remotely follow the syllabus, as she skipped entire modules and taught topics out of order. She seldom provided any feedback on grades, making it unclear why we received the grades that we did. Due these countless issues, me and my peers oftentimes had to email her for clarification on assignments. However, she never responded to emails despite insisting that we should never hesitate to reach out to her via email outside of class. She is easily the worst professor I have ever had in my entire life.", "The instructor could consider making the homework assignments more challenging and application–oriented.Adding office hours and TA support would provide students with more opportunities for guidance.Organizing the course schedule more clearly could also help.Making the course material more practical and application–oriented would be helpful and including more in–class assignments where students collaborate to solve data science problems could improve engagement and teamwork.", "This was a very poorly run course from top to bottom. It's better if I make a list. I'm sorry to be harsh, Professor Nafa is a very nice woman, but she is not meant to be a professor. My time was wasted for the most part this summer on this course when it could have been great with even the tiniest attention to detail and care.1. There was no communication in between lectures. Once lecture was over, you would not hear from her until next week.2. There was no predetermined plan for any lecture. She would sit down, open her laptop, and decide what we would do that day. We would start the lecture around 15 minutes after 6.3. Assignments were created on the spot during lecture half the time, contributing to wasting our time during each lecture. Some preparation beforehand would have been an easy fix!4. We spent a lot of time in lecture watching her code and debug. If something wasn't working, she would go to ChatGPT and sometimes spend a really long time there until she came up with a result. For two of the lectures, it was around 2 hours of sitting there wasting our time. Some preparation beforehand would have also been an easy fix!5. When lecturing, she would read directly from slides and didn't seem to know how to explain beyond what was written on the slide. Some of our classmates found out that she had taken her slides from online/youtube videos.6. Our assignments and quizzes took around 15 minutes each to complete on average. No real learning was done doing simple SELECT FROM queries and pandas filtering.7. When she cancels a lecture, she doesn't give you anything to do. It was two weeks of radio silence. Not even anything to read.8. She didn't assign any readings after the first week other than some lengthy research papers one week. There was no textbook and you needed to find the materials yourself for all the self teaching you needed.9. Syllabus was not followed at all. We were supposed to have readings, weekly assignments, in class assignments, STRUCTURE. What we actually had to do was a small fraction of all that. The project was the only beef of the course and you need to dedicate a lot of time to that to actually learn anything.10. Canvas was a mess. It was not organized at all and really hard to follow as she would randomly through things on there. There were barely any learning materials there, just lecture slides and assignments/data files.11. Attendance was strictly mandatory, despite the class having no substance. It was frustrating being forced to be there just to have most of our time wasted watching her fumble around figuring out what to teach. You made attendance mandatory because you know that no one will show up if not. I could have not shown up for each lecture and potentially learned even more on my own in those 3 hours.12.  Other iterations of this course focus on teaching you R, but this class was fully in Python. Would have been nice to see some more real world tools. We spent essentially one month on basic SQL. A 3rd grader can do basic SQL. We could have at least upped the intensity of it. Then we spent a lot of time on PySpark. This part of the course was good, but only if you put in the effort to self teach it. The rest of the course was high level overviews into Data Science processes like ML", "Please prepare your lessons more thoroughly!"]

comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Homework felt a little on the theoretical side sometimes", "SMALL CHANGES– A more energetic tone in lecture just to keep students engaged– A teams/piazza workspace where students could engage with each other offline or ask questions for everyone to see– More discussions on the practical relevance of these techniques and how their use could change depending on the contextBIG CHANGES1) More assignments over shorter periods of time. We had 3 homeworks worth 30% of our grade. 2 of these weren't assigned until the last 3 weeks of class. For the first 2 months of class I had only done 1 assignment which was a day's worth of work. Yet the material itself is difficult and full of lots to learn about in both quiz/theory format and practical exercises. I would have benefited more from 7–10 homeworks or quizzes. Early trial and error is crucial for determining if your students follow your teaching or not.2)Because of point #1, I also think it was a mistake to require students to submit their project proposal after they've only had 1 assignment. The course project is an estimated 40–60hrs of work. We had only studied 30–40% of the curriculum (the teacher canceled 3 classes this semester) so we were already behind when proposals were due. The project guidelines suggested topics that we hadnt covered, or even in–depth exploration of topics beyond the scope of the class. Yet we had only done ONE assignment in this course that we could use as the basis of our expertise. It would have been better to either a) require proposals much later when students have better mastery of the topics, or b) assign a default project based on one consistent subject already studied in class and practiced in assignments, but at a much higher scale of complexity and freedom of expression.3) We didnt have a midterm (again we were behind in the course), so we only had one exam (our final) worth 30% of our grade. The weight distribution was so heavy towards that final that missing 3 multiple choice questions in the final exam meant that 12 multiple choice questions were worth 12% of our entire grade in the class. I would suggest a rebalancing of weights that doesn't result in such a make–or–break situations for a set of multiple questions.4) A more efficient use of time spent. Because of cancelled classes, we have items on the course syllabus we didn't end up covering at all.", "Can improve the assignment time"]

course_info: - TRACE report for ENGW1111-02 First-Year Writing  (Matthew Hitchcock)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["It's already the best.", "Include more examples of past student work.", "Get to the point quicker in videos. Often one gets completely lost and misses the main point entirely. It would also have been nice to have some feedback on my work rather than just a grade without context, since when people put effort into their work, they probably prefer to get at least a few comments.", "Nothing– it was truly an excellent experience", "I feel Mr. Hitchcock sincerely tried his best at crafting a good course. Your effort is much appreciated! and there are only a few pieces of feedback I have to offer.First, I feel like some of the concepts seemed pretty obvious. For example, looking at the IB curriculum, students are asked to examine how multiple modes of communication, from the layout of texts down to how and where banners are placed, interact with their context of creation and reception, and make sophisticated analysis on the method, message, purpose, etc. just check out litlearn's method of teaching this, trust me. https://litlearn.com/Another thing to talk about are Georgia Lupi's ideas. Are altering the color scheme of a graphic and creating an arbitrary music video really the best ways to communicate a child's tragic situation??? Here a well–written story with curated images would have not only sufficed, but they would have far outshined the approach that Lupi took. Don't get me wrong, Lupi's philosophy of challenging traditional ways of using data is profound, but her interpretation of that seems to be \"if it's traditional, there's no way it's going to be used\", even if traditional data presentation methods may have been more effective at communicating the message.Lastly, notice Lupi does not process the data at all. She is simply copying the exact same data to a piece of paper, clothing, or other medium. To make an analogy, it's like writing a research paper simply by pasting all the photos, research results, and citations onto a word document and calling it a day. This is clearly not very productive. If that was not bad enough, she then encodes this copy using an arbitrary language. Squares, triangles, circles, dots, and colors are regular components of her language, which often don't have any correlation whatsoever with her projects, for example the programs from Ada Lovelace. Like why choose a circle for this part of the program?? In fact, it seemed that she actively avoided intuitive designs. \"I always make sure to include the legend, such that the viewer can only read the project with it\". She goes the extra mile to add an extra layer of complexity. Does this not go against the entire purpose of data visualization?! Ultimately, not only does Lupi fail to extract insights from data, she makes the data even less valuable and influential by reducing its readability.", "n/a", "Nothing. It was great!", "I wish he had warned us when we should have started working on our projects if we were sharing them.", "Maybe this is just me, but a bit more guidance on what each project should be. comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["I think program administration should audit our course and review Prof. Prevost's communications with students. It's clear that she has no intention of treating students with dignity and respect, which is ironic considering the subject matter of this course.", "While I never met professor Prevost in person or over zoom, I did very much enjoy her class and the way it was formatted. One thing that stood out to me was after my groups this semester submitted our case study assignments, she took the time to look it over and quickly send us and email telling us we did well and not to worry. I thought this was really sweet and definitely helped me to relax a little bit while awaiting my grades for these assignments. I really liked how the case studies were done and feel as though they directly related to nursing school and what I could experience in my future career as a nurse. I also loved how this course was centered around real stories of events that actually happened and affected people's lives as well as policy in regard to nursing practice. It made learning this content way more interesting than if we were just reading about concepts. One suggestion I have for this would be to give a bit more time for students to respond to their peer's presentations; I like to get all of my schoolwork done at the beginning of the week as I work the weekends and enjoy taking a break from schoolwork during this time. With the case study presentations being due on Thursdays and responses being due Sunday, I often had to do work on Friday/Saturday/Sunday– not a huge deal, but I would have liked this to be structured a bit differently. Maybe in the future, presentations could be due earlier in the week to give students time to get their responses done prior to the weekend? Another thing I noticed about this course was the syllabus– it was quite unorganized and difficult to read. I didn't like how there was information for the same assignment in both the assignment and the syllabus but it wasn't the same in both places? It felt like going on a scavenger hunt trying to figure out how to do assignments– in the future, please put all of the information for an assignment in the assignment rather than the syllabus to make things easier for everyone. I think another read–through of the syllabus by the professors for this course could have avoided these issues. Overall, professor Prevost was very kind when I spoke to her via email or in the discussion forums and I enjoyed working with her this semester!"]

comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["I liked that the case study assignments were on zoom so we could facilitate discussions. The material was organized well and the instructor was typically responsive by email.", "I do not mean this as a comment on my professor, because she is not really responsible. The structure of the course – old, prerecorded lectures and outdated videos online– is ridiculous. There is no \"teaching\" in these classes. The only way to do well is to utilize the textbook and the textbook website. The assignments and lectures were actually a waste of time. The tests and quizzes are based off of the textbook, and therefore they are extremely challenging questions that are easy to misinterpret and VERY confusing. There is no guidance on how to navigate this, you just have to hope that you saw similar questions in the Davis website quizzes. I did reasonably well on the tests and still left them frustrated because of how unclear and confusing the questions on the tests are.  I found that if I watched the lectures, I did worse than if I didn't, because they are old and do not align with the material being taught in the book – they put emphasis on things that we are not tested on. It was extremely challenging to figure out what to learn– which is unfair to the students. Online meetings with my professor were helpful in that she told us what to focus on. Without those pointers I would have been completely lost. The assignments were useful only if you scoured the textbook for the answers (which takes longer than just reading the chapters) but overall they did not help in my comprehension of the material because there was no final \"key\" or explanation to study. We went through it in a zoom meeting with student participation, which means that you had to try to sift through the other students answers to correct your own during the meeting. It's not helpful.Also, the syllabus is clearly copied from another year or edition of the book and the page numbers are wrong. This was very annoying to discover. More frustrating was the fact that the only way to learn the material is by studying chapter quizzes through the online platform. However, the pages assigned for each module would be partial chapters (e.g. pages 611–612) but you can't utilize the online resources that way, you have to study the whole chapter. This was frustrating.", "This course was organized very well and easy to navigate through all the modules.", "It was good for an online course but you never really interacted with other students.", "This class needs to have a zoom meeting every week NOT just a case study where you feel like you are under rapid fire at anytime but do not have to be there if there is clinical's at the same time. The information by the instructor, the book, davis and the tests do not align. There is NO way that someone can read over all of the information 5 times (which is what the instructor says we need to do in order to be successful) as well as devote 3–5 hours per DAY to just this class alone. The expectations were not clearly defined but very much expected at an unattainable and unrealistic level. Just because I get a \"bad\" score on an assignment, does not mean that I did not study or try my hardest, which is another thing stated by the instructor.", "This course was very easy to navigate and all the needed information could be found within the weekly modules. It was overall very straightforward and I always knew what was expected of me."]

comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Could become. abit more flexible when garding hw", "The case studies should NOT be graded off of a key. If you have the right idea and it is technically correct then you should get the points. I spent hours on these case studies and felt my grades did not reflect it", "I feel like they focused on real world practice rather than prepared us for what is going to be asked of us on exams.  One instance in particular, this instructor held an exam practice question session and asked a question about priority nursing action for a patient with  COPD exacerbation. The answer was administer O2, then a student asked why it wasn't a SABA and the instructor explained their rationale and doubled down that the answer was still oxygen. A similar question appeared on the exam with oxygen and SABA both being choices and oxygen ended up being the wrong one. This instructor also would take points off of case studies for things they said were wrong even though I pulled the information directly from the textbook with the textbook cited as an in–text citation. After asking students in other classes and sections that had similar answers to my own, they reported that they got it right with full credit. I feel overall there needs to be clear expectations communicated across all course faculty, because there were things said in the provided lectures that are wrong. For example one of the lectures asked nursing priority intervention for a stroke patient and said getting IV access should be done before a head CT, but when that same question was asked in the case study head CT was more priority over IV access. Its just confusing for students to be getting no clear cut, consistent answer and makes every exam and case study feel like a guessing game of which answer the staff feels is going to be correct today."]

comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["This course was extremely disorganized. The book and the syllabus did NOT line up with what we were learning. The instructor was great but this class was extremely difficult to navigate withe the little resources they provided. The powerpoint lectures were pathetic. For a one credit class , the lectures should've contained notes not just a voiceover. This meant that to take notes you had to stop the video ever 5 seconds to write stuff down. Also you had to search through the book for information that the lectures didn't cover which was ridiculous because the book chapters didn't line up with the syllabus. We informed the instructor and others and they provided no help.", "I don't always feel that this course corresponded with the lab component appropriately to adequately prepare us for exams.", "I felt that I did not have the level of support I needed in this course. Office hours were held during my clinical time, which made it difficult for me to access additional help. The lectures often did not provide enough clarification, and the textbook readings were too broad and did not align closely with the focus of the exam questions. While I really enjoyed the subject matter, I believe I could have learned more effectively if the material had been presented in a more structured and targeted way. My prior work experience gave me a helpful foundation, but without that background, I would have felt much more lost and confused throughout the course.", "This course was poorly organized by the school. The lectures offered few slides with pictures and little to no context about the material being learned. I understand it is an asynchronous course but even when asking my course facility about content they too were lost. On different occasions, I voiced to my instructor that office hours conflicted with my clinical schedule and they would not record the office hour for me nor provide any supplemental material to help study. Instructor said study guides \"were not allowed\" and we were not well prepared for exams. Considering how this is a course with 5 total exams it should be reviewed by school of nursing to better prepare future students enrolled.", "This course lacked support, appropriate resources, and organization of material, which negatively impacted my learning and ability to be successful."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-B01 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Melissa Palumbo)

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-B03 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Daniel Conway)
comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["The PowerPoint slides are very poor and not informative at all. Majority of the PowerPoint slides are pictures with no information on them. If there wasn’t a book this class would be impossible with just the PowerPoints.", "Online course lectures are incredibly superficial in content and often just consist of photos lifted from Lippincott and also they do little to emphasize what content provided in the text is important for exams or assignments. As an example, our documentation case study asks us to identify 5 framework elements of nursing documentation, which are not provided in the module, in Lippincott, nor on Kaplan. It also asks us about EMTALA which is similarly not covered in course materials. For these assignments I felt that the only way to possibly answer them correctly was to use Google, which we are advised not to do at the beginning of the semester.Our entire class also had a lot of difficulty with the second exam; in my section, the class average was a 68 out of 100 before the exam was regraded. We were provided with a 10 minute video that I think ended up confusing many students, or led us to believe that some content wasn't appropriate for this course (e.g. the video told us that we would never be removing a chest tube, so they omitted how; meanwhile the exam had a question on how to guide a patient through a chest tube removal). The exam occurred before we covered chest tubes in lab. My section of lab is directly after the exam and it was defeating to see all the students' frustrations when we realized how many of the questions we had answered incorrectly because we had insufficient access to learning materials. From talking to 3rd and 4th semester students, this seems like a long standing problem and I think it would be worthwhile to gather data on whether or not this is an issue with all campuses, or just the Burlington campus.Another issue that I thought was bizarre was that there is no Lippincott course for this class like there was with 2220. To access our textbook and skills videos, we had to go into the archived course content from last semester. I know that the practice and learns for skills covered in this course are optional, but I would have appreciated if they were assigned for no credit just to make it a bit easier for us to find appropriate content while we are studying for exams. I don't know what the rationale was for not making a course in Lippincott, but from our end it feels cheap and lazy."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-B04 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Daniel Conway)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["I think the entire course faculty should redo the module slides. They do not contain enough information and lack detail. All there is on the slides is pictures, which are great, but they need more information, or the speaker needs to include more information.", "Overall, this course was a waste of time. For each exam, I watched every Kaplan video, every lippincott video, read all assigned pages of the chapters, and took notes on the powerpoints slides as instructed to do so by the syllabus. When exams would come around the formatting was horrific like someone had copied the exam from another site and put it into canvas. Questions were not legible, gave no question sometimes, and were hardly ever on the content we were instructed to study. This semester was truly discouraging in this class. Professors should not receive emails from almost all of us after exams stating that isn't what we learned and continuously do nothing about it the entire semester. I think something really needs to change in this course because it is awful to see that this is how our education is being treated by faculty.", "So to preface this I want to point out that I am doing well in this class and will probably get an A, I even did  good on the exam that everyone did poorly on (exam 2) so this review does not come from a place of bitterness.With that being said, this class in an absolute joke.It is a 1 credit class with 5 exams, in what universe is that appropriate?? I thought credit hours were supposed to equate to hours spent working. In this program we spend the majority of our time (not all of it, but the majority) making study guides and studying for exams. Therefore, I spent almost as much time on this class as I did in my 4 credit classes.Exam 4 takes place the week before finals. I will ask the same question as above: in what universe if that appropriate? Especially for a 1 credit class. Because of this scheduling I lost an entire week of studying for finals so I could study for an exam in a 1 credit class. Awesome.So it's a 1 credit class with 5 exams, the exams must be a little easier than in a 4 credit class right? Nope. The exams in this class are riddled with overly tricky questions and/or answers. I would argue that the exams for this (1 credit) class were the hardest of any class I have taken thus far in this program.I am not done talking about exams but I will take a break from that area for a moment.Module 2 in this class is \"Advanced Head to Toe Assessment: Cranial Nerves & Advanced Cardiovascular Assessment\". Please tell me why half of the assigned readings for this module (Ch. 26 pgs. 716–721) was intro to vital signs and temperature? We already covered this material in the first semester. When I asked the professors about this they didn't really have much of an answer (through no fault of their ownbecause they don't make the syllabus nor do they see the exam before we take it) but kind of said that vital signs are important to know. Well yeah of course they are. Based on this rationale we should be tested on vital signs for every exam in every class.Alright back to exams. Over the past 2 years I have taken probably 100 exams across 20 classes. Out of all of those exams only the ones taken in this class contained material from OTHER classes or material that wasn't covered in the modules that the exam was on.Some examples:Exam 1 on Therapeutic Communication, Advanced Cardiovascular Assessment, and Cranial Nerves had a random question on abdominal assessment.Exam 2 on PIV & CVAD, Advanced Respiratory (Artifical Airway, Tracheostomy & Suctioning), and Chest Tubes had this question about some obscure breath sound that even Google couldn't give me a definitive answer on.Exam 4 on Advanced Elimination and Documentation had 4 questions (10% of the exam!) on dialysis. Was dialysis mentioned once in the measly PowerPoint presentation? No.  Were we explicitly told in the syllabus to stop reading BEFORE \"Caring for a Patient with Dialysis\"? Yes. Will someone in the background that we've never met twist themselves in a knot rationalizing the inclusion of these dialysis questions on the exam? Probably.Per the professors these random inclusions on the exam are things we should know as nursing students. An \"audit\" was done on exam 2 and it was found that only 1 question contained material that wasn't covered in the PowerPoint and/or the assigned readings. Give me a break. There is a 0% chance that this is accurate. I imagine this \"audit\" was done by the person who created the exam.I am horrified to think what is going to be included on the final. Will there be material from our maternity class? Will we be tested on state capitals or the Bill of Rights? These are things we should know aren't they?If I had to sum this class up in 1 sentece it would be: The exams are not given in good faith."]

It seemed she mainly wanted us to read the textbook and teach ourselves.", "Great learning experience in how tests are worded and how to do better on each exam"]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-N01 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Veronica Rankin)
comment_prompt: What were the strengths of this course and/or this instructor?
comments_json: ["none", "The course was not organized the best, but I really appreciated how Dr. Rankin interacted and taught course material during the “Touchbase” meetings every few weeks. It was obvious that she wanted her students to do well and the PowerPoints she referenced were neatly organized. When trying to prepare for the second exam of the semester, it was challenging. Students were not given specific detailed information. For example, I could not find in the textbook where it stated a patient is able to lay on the same side of their chest tube, but this was a test question. Also, for the final exam, for it being 25% of over overall grade, but only having 40 questions, I thought was a bit unfair. Furthermore, students were not provided a study guide, which I believe is a bare minimum thing to supply students, especially for a heavily weighted exam.", "The strengths of this instructor was that she attended sessions very enthusiastically and was well–prepared, organized, and a great teacher. I do appreciate the time that this instructor puts into her Zooms!", "N/A", "The teacher did a great job communicating and helping me understand concepts when I needed help. comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["The online course environment was supportive, and the instructor did an excellent job creating an engaging and positive learning atmosphere. I truly appreciated her teaching style and commitment to student success. That said, I noticed a gap between the provided course content and the material emphasized on exams. Since the instructor did not design the course structure or assessments, this seems to be a curriculum issue rather than an instructional one. Aligning lectures, learning resources, and assessments more closely would greatly enhance the effectiveness of the course and ensure that students can demonstrate their knowledge more accurately.", "n/a", "Overall, I had a great experience in the online course environment. It correlated with our Lab.", "The course I felt went hand and hand with lab. This is where we gained the understanding from the text to applying it in lab."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-N03 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Veronica Rankin)
comment_prompt: What were the strengths of this course and/or this instructor?
comments_json: ["The greatest strength of this course was the instructor. She was knowledgeable, approachable, and deeply committed to student success. She consistently went above and beyond, including correcting misinformation from lab instructors to ensure we had the most accurate understanding. Her clear explanations and supportive teaching style made a challenging subject much more approachable and built my confidence as a student nurse. Honorable mention also goes to the course coordinator, Professor Gleaton, whose guidance and support further contributed to a positive learning experience.", "n/a", "Professor Rankin always came prepared to the touch base sessions. She had a way of explaining things that made sense and was very engaging. I enjoyed and looked forward to either attending her touch base sessions live or by the recordings."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-N03 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Veronica Rankin)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["The instructor was excellent and very effective in her role, so there is little I would change. The main improvements needed are at the course design level rather than her teaching. If anything, providing even more opportunities for review sessions or practice questions that mirror the exam format could further strengthen the learning experience. Overall, she already goes above and beyond to support students, and the areas for improvement are more related to course content alignment than to her instruction.", "n/a", "This is nothing against the Professor Rankin herself as she consistently prepared us thoroughly for each exam with her informational touch bases sessions and open to 1:1 meetings to clarify content material, etc.After Exam 2, however, the announcement stated, \"All sections of this course for the Charlotte, Burlington, and Boston campuses saw a major dip on Exam #2 as compared to Exam #1. This dip was also seen last semester on Exam 2, but Exam 3 grades were exceptionally high.\"With that said, it is clear that this is not an isolated issue, as the drop in grades occurred across multiple campuses and in previous semesters.The exam itself was simply very challenging. This was especially concerning because this course is solely based on exam grades all being 15% of the final grade and the final being weighted 25%, meaning that every exam carries significant weight toward the final grade. For example, I scored a 45 on Exam 2, despite putting in significant effort and studying thoroughly.On Exam 3, however, I earned a 95. This drastic difference was not due to a lack of preparation or hard work on Exam 2, but rather reflects the difficulty and nature of that particular assessment/exam.While I understand that most courses have at least one particularly difficult exam per semester, I was disappointed that there was no opportunity for remediation, extra credit, or even a slight curve to help address the impact. Given the recurring pattern from previous semesters across multiple campuses, I feel it would have been beneficial for faculty across the campuses to explore proactive solutions to help students better manage and recover from this specific assessment."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-N03 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Veronica Rankin)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["The instructor excelled at creating an inclusive online learning environment. She was approachable, attentive, and fully engaged during weekly office hours, ensuring that all students felt supported and heard. She welcomed diverse perspectives and encouraged participation from everyone, fostering a safe and equitable space for learning. Her dedication to student engagement, respect, and fairness made the course exceptionally positive and effective.", "n/a", "The instructor demonstrated strong strengths in facilitating inclusive learning by ensuring that all students had opportunities to prepare for exams through informational touch base sessions, as well as offering 1:1 meetings to clarify content and address individual questions. She created a supportive learning environment where students felt comfortable seeking help and engaging with course material."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3323-N03 Adv Assess & Interventions  (Veronica Rankin)
comment_prompt: What I could have done to make this course better for myself.
comments_json: ["N/A", "n/a", "After the first exam, I realized that my study method needed to be more active and hands–on. I discovered that physically writing down my notes, rather than typing them, was far more effective for my retention and understanding of the material.To take this a step further, I began rewriting my notes three times and by the third time, I would challenge myself to recall definitions, concepts, and topics entirely from memory without looking back at the original notes. This repetitive recall process not only reinforced the information but also highlighted any gaps in my understanding that I could then go back and review.In addition to note rewriting, I incorporated about 60–100 NCLEX–style application–based practice questions into my study routine for each exam. I would continue working through these questions until I consistently scored 90% or higher.This approach was incredibly helpful because it trained me to think critically, apply knowledge in clinical scenarios, and identify patterns in question styles. By combining the memorization benefits of repeated note writing with the application skills from practice questions, I entered each exam feeling significantly more confident and prepared.", "Payed attention more to the content, and diving deeper into the text for clarification on even the \"simple\" things that I may have gotten wrong."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3420-B01 Nursing Care of Adults 2  (Gretchen Forsley)
comment_prompt: What were the strengths of this course and/or this instructor?
comments_json: ["Dr. Forsley is very enthusiastic about teaching, and it shows when we are online and synchronous. However, there were just TOO many slides in the pre–recorded lectures. It is so overwhelming and so much additional information and detail that is just not needed. Dr. Forsley is very effective at teaching, but she loses me in the long lectures that are not engaging. I enjoyed the office hours and additional case study zooms, and I wish that's how she taught because the prerecorded lectures just are not as effective. It's a shame because I feel like I could have learned so much more and had a better time understanding and engaging in the material if the pre–recorded lectures were not just instructors reading off a script. It feels so disconnected and unhelpful. I always have to turn to YouTube channels because the pre–recorded lectures are not as engaging, and the visuals are just not as helpful.", "Dr. Forsley is invested in her students and wants everyone to have the knowledge and skills to succeed. I feel more prepared as a result of this course."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3420-B01 Nursing Care of Adults 2  (Gretchen Forsley)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Host more structured zoom meetings like the case studies, where we can go over practice questions and engage in the class. Also, the lectures were too long, filled with too much information, which made studying overwhelming.", "I think that it would be helpful for students to choose their own groups for the exam review presentations. Accelerated students have varied schedules and it is really difficult to work with people that you do not know well. For example, a few of my group members preferred to complete assignments last minute which does not work for my school or personal schedule and I communicated that at the beginning of the semester. The result was that two members of our group had to frontload work in order to ensure that the assignments could be submitted before the deadline if we did not hear from our other group members."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG3420-B01 Nursing Care of Adults 2  (Gretchen Forsley)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["This is out of the instructor's control but improvements to the exam process are needed. I had issues getting into each exam and lost up to 3 minutes each time navigating in and out the exam in order for it to properly load. We also had an exam where the wrong time was set and the exam timed out before we could finish. I know that our professors put significant effort into creating fair exams to assess our knowledge and the result was not fair to the faculty or students. The technical issues that we experienced this semester were beyond frustrating and the exam administrator needs to address these issues immediately, including providing workarounds and a troubleshooting guide for their employees."]

comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["A lot of students often felt the study guides were unhelpful as they lacked information and clarity. We were often tested on material that was not on the study guide. On one of the exams, there was like 3 powerpoints on this one subject,  making it seem like a very big and important topic, but then we had very few questions about it on the exam. Then we had like 6 questions on a topic that had like 4 slides in one powerpoint, making it seem not super important and we didn't have much information on it to go off of.The self–assessment quizzes were helpful most of the time, but they usually lacked context/ scenario information that would have helped answer the questions. For example (that I am making up), you would get the question wrong and then in the reasoning it would give you an explanation that would make sense if you had the background information and you're left thinking  \"well how did I know it was a 3 year–old boy with pneumonia and not a 14 year–old girl with diabetes if they never said\" – cause that would change your answer if it was about educating the child for example.", "Another lecturer redoing the lectures to focus on more nursing interventions would be helpful. Not having office hours right before our exams would be helpful as well."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG4604-B02 Public Health Community Nursng  (Jessica Humphrey)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["I never met this professor. Her office hours were inaccessible to me due to clinical placements and work obligations and she was not flexible in this aspect. The AI interview assignment was weird and could have used a lot more instruction for how it was going to work. It was very clear that the syllabus was made by the faculty in Boston, rather than those in Burlington. I understand that the courses between Boston and Burlington are similar however it did not at all feel like the syllabus was read through by this professor. There were countless typos and incorrect assignment dates, to a point where it felt like the syllabus was copied and pasted from another course and the instructor just slapped her name and email on it. There were many instances in the syllabus where is said \"we'll talk about this in class\" we never had synchronous meetings so this made no sense. For as much as I've paid for this program, it is frustrating that a professor couldn't even proofread and edit the syllabus for this course– especially because they did not even have to put the syllabus for their course together themselves. The students in this course should not have had to reach out to ask clarifying questions for EVERY assignment (we all talk!) and the syllabus and assignment guidelines should have been abundantly more clear. I very much enjoyed the content of the public health course but it was organized poorly. I also did not like how our quizzes didn't line up with the most recent modules. It was confusing to have a quiz on modules 8 and 9 when we were finishing content on module 10; this could have been set–up better. Also, why did we have to come to campus for a quiz taking less than 30 minutes? Some of us live an hour plus from campus and it was annoying to have to come all the way there for less than 30 minutes, especially when we were forced to download a lockdown browser at the beginning of this program that I never used! These could easily be online to save time, money, and aggravation for the students. It took FOREVER to get assignment grades back for this course, way longer than any other course I have ever taken in this program or in my undergraduate degree prior to this. If students are held to deadlines, professors should be too. I should not be waiting weeks for assignment grades. I hope you will take this into account for the next semester of this course– it is not fair to the students for things to be this disorganized. Like I said previously, I enjoyed the topic of public health and probably would have liked the course itself a lot more had the professor taken the initiative to organize herself and the course."]

comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["I was very surprised to see that Dr. McCadden was my professor for this course after she retired midway through our first semester of this program with little warning. I enjoyed the assignments this semester for the most part and didn't feel like they were busy work. I do wish Dr. McCadden and the other professor for this course communicated better– there were a few instances in which the dates between courses did not line up. This made it confusing for students as the norm for this program is that no matter what section of the course you are in, all of the assignments and due dates were the same. I also believe the syllabus should have been reviewed and edited prior to the start of this course. It was clear that the Boston campus created the syllabus and the Burlington campus \"adapted\" it– I use quotes because this was unfortunately not done well. I also wish I would have had the opportunity to choose my own group and topic for the capstone project. I was lucky and enjoyed the group I was assigned to work with, however, all of us agreed that we would have chosen a different topic if given the opportunity. Maybe in the future it would be helpful to allow students to choose their own group and topic and then assign groups/topics as needed to students who didn't have one? Also, there was a ton of group work in all courses this semester, making it difficult for me to keep track of who was in which group for what class. I think it could have been beneficial for the professors for this semester to talk to each other when assigning groups and maybe assign one group for ALL group work across courses to make things a little less complicated."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG4996-B01 Clinical for NRSG 4995  (Nancy Mizzoni)
comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.
comments_json: ["Nancy is such a nice and supportive person, I feel really lucky to have been able to have her as a resource throughout this program. I really appreciated that she was on campus most days while I was there for presentations, sims, and exams– in a program where there is little in–person collaboration, it was nice to know that she was the one constant that would be there. Whether or not she had you as a student, I saw Nancy extend her kindness and knowledge to all. She wasn't always organized and she didn't always have the answers, but she was ALWAYS willing to work with her students to figure it out together– in my opinion, this is what really matters. I can't say enough good things about Dr. Mizzoni and feel as though she is a wonderful resource to all attending the Burlington ABSN."]

comments_json: ["Overall fantastic.", "She is clearly very knowledgeable about epidemiology", "Dr. Damus was respectful to everyone and extremely helpful when extra help was requested whether that was in class or outside class. comment_prompt: What were the strengths of this course and/or this instructor?
comments_json: ["This course was simply busy work. Everyone in this program has graduated from undergrad. In undergrad, I wrote two formal research proposals and completed a formal thesis for my senior project, where I planned, executed, and wrote up a study that I conducted myself. Starting my freshman year during undergrad, I started learning how to read scientific papers and journals and how to analyze them. This entire course was busy work, and it would have been better to have just covered the ethics course now.  I understand the course was taken over a little bit into the semester, but the change of expectations and changes in due dates to the syllabus were difficult to incorporate, as the group had already made arrangements and schedules on childcare and work days around the original syllabus dates. Again, I cannot stress how useless this class was, and it is no reflection on the professor; this course did not teach me anything new and just took my time away from the other courses.", "Dr. Monagle was available and open to meeting with her students. Meetings were productive and helpful in improving the proposal."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG5220-B01 Intro Research Method Hlthcare  (Janet Monagle)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Please incorporate more individual assignments or allow us to provide anonymous peer feedback at the end of each assignment that will count towards our grade. One of my group members literally did NO work all semester. If there was an incentive to do well (per feedback that affects your grade), I feel like the participation would have been better.", "We had a change in professor mid semester and I think that it would have been helpful to have a zoom meeting at this transition to discussion changes in expectations, including assignment due dates. For example, the feedback on drafts was changed in format (assigned versus discussion board) and from group to individual. My group had already decided on who was drafting each feedback assignment for the rest of the group to review and the first feedback was drafted before we learned about the change in format. This resulted in additional work for the rest of my group that week. The accelerated nature of our program means that we have tight schedules and last minute changes are difficult to maneuver. Additionally, due dates for final assignments were changed after this transition (two weeks following) and I would have appreciated more time to plan."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG5220-B02 Intro Research Method Hlthcare  (Corinne Fiory-Eanes)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["One thing I didn't like was the group work for the class. It seems that each section/class has a similar opinion on this and is not just Professor Fiory–Eanes section. I think that it is unfair that some teachers allowed for students to pick their group members and some were automatically assigned. It is surprising that even in an accelerated program there are still students who wait till the last minute, don’t contribute, don't try, and don't communicate. I think it’s unfair to be placed in a group where these types of students are present. I think it is unfair because we all get the same grade. No matter how hard you try, there is never an equal workload for the group. A way to fix this is I recommend to have all these writing assignments be independent work or let students pick their groups.", "i think its ok for the most part, we had a lot to write but that's what school is for, not easy but we got through it.", "let us pick our groups, other sections were allowed too", "These assignments were very repetitive. And most of the things for the data we have to make up values and ideas which is great to know the process but for quantitative studies it is hard to come up with those ideas. By the end when doing the final paper it just felt like we were writing the same things time and time again and did not feel necessary."]

comment_prompt: Please expand on the instructor’s strengths and/or areas for improvement in facilitating inclusive learning.

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG5220-N01 Intro Research Method Hlthcare  (Kristy Rogers)
comment_prompt: Please comment on your experience of the online course environment in the open-ended text box.
comments_json: ["This course is a great way to develop a good understanding of writing, team work, and professional papers. However, like the real world you really have to pray for a good group pairing to have an easy flow through the course. I believe being online gives the group more autonomy for when group work needs to be completed however, I feel like there should be a requirement of in person meetings to ensure everyone in the group has the same expectations and understanding of roles. This course has taught me many individuals have different writing styles which can impact a flow of paper and there is a great portion of our cohort that struggles/doesn't understand APA, regardless of resources.", "The grading for the course was VERY inconsistent between my peers and I. Rubrics were unclear and contained conflicting information on page limit requirements (two different page limits depending on which rubric you looked at). We submitted drafts for feedback before we got final grades from the instructor, and the drafts were not read, so the final assignment grade suffered for that. I would advise in the future to not have students turn in a draft for feedback as a submission to improve if the feedback will not be given.", "This class felt like the ultimate waste of time.  Having group assignments on a weekly basis did not foster a sense of community it only fostered a sense of frustration.  Yes, we need to learn to work as a team but when your grade depends on your classmates it is really hard to find the patience for people who don't seem to have time for school work.  This class was poorly designed and did not help me learn anything about team work, writing or research. I did not watch a lecture or read a book for this class and I still got a very good grade.  It would be great to understand the goal of this class and rework it to something that feels like it is needed,", "na", "It was difficult to feel community with a group project class in an online platform. I think this class should meet once a week in person, to have time in person with group."]

course_info: - TRACE report for NRSG5220-N01 Intro Research Method Hlthcare  (Kristy Rogers)
comment_prompt: What could the instructor do to make this course better?
comments_json: ["Regarding assignment expectations, an example is that the rubric stated a 2–3 page limit, yet feedback indicated we needed to include two paragraphs for a specific section per the rubric. This creates confusion—if we adhere to the page limit, it’s difficult to meet the paragraph requirement; but if we expand to meet the paragraph requirement, we exceed the stated limit. It’s unclear whether the 2–3 pages is intended as a strict limit or more of a suggested minimum.I also believe this course would work better as an individual–assignment course. If it is to remain a group–based course, students should be allowed to choose their own group members if they already have a group in mind. Many students reported not being able to reach their group members until close to the deadline, which is unfair to those who complete their work in a timely manner. Additionally, coordinating schedules among group members in a hybrid ABSN program is especially challenging, as many students cannot meet synchronously. This makes it difficult to write a paper collaboratively in real time, leaving most groups to divide sections and compile them at the end. Providing a short PowerPoint or guide on effectively composing a paper under these circumstances—accounting for varying schedules and availability—would help set clear expectations and improve group outcomes.", "I would suggest having groups meet in person/online meetings at least 1 for each group portion of the project so everyone is on the same page, or keeping track of what person is doing what within a group, many groups had individuals that chose easy portions or didn't do a lot of work which I think should contribute to their grade.", "see comments above", "Help us understand why this course is needed and not a waste of time.  Setting us up into groups where some folks don't participate just creates frustration.", "na"]

comments_json: ["I appreciate students being asked to expand on their thought processes; however, the comments that followed from Josh were harsh, occasionally non–constructive, and condescending. Pushing us to all engage in conversation is one thing, but saying something like \"student A, I'm not going to call you out...\" and then proceeding to very directly call them out and diminish their thought process, while we are meant to be LEARNING, is not helpful. Nor does it facilitate an inclusive and comfortable environment for students to share thoughts/experiences, ask questions, and engage with peers and instructors. Again, Josh seems to have great experience and is passionate about learning/teaching, but the delivery is not conducive to a productive learning environment.  I am certain that each one of my peers agrees.I think he could be a really great instructor if he is able to change his delivery."]
